preFix.title: Swedish lussekatter
preFix.subtitle: Saffron-scented S-shaped buns for Saint Lucia
preFix.excerpt: Lussekatter are the saffron buns baked throughout Sweden in December for Saint Lucia Day on the 13th. Each bun is rolled into a long rope, then coiled at each end to form a double S. The saffron dyes the dough a vivid yellow and perfumes the crumb. They are enriched with butter and egg, soft and slightly sweet.
preFix.sourceNotes: Lussekatter (Lucia cats) are documented as a Swedish Saint Lucia tradition from at least the seventeenth century, though the double-S or lyre shape became standard in the nineteenth. The name may derive from Lucifer (light bringer) or Lucia herself. Saffron was an expensive imported spice in Scandinavian baking, associated with celebration and festival breads. The basic recipe of saffron, milk, butter, and yeast has remained largely unchanged.

content.text: The saffron must be bloomed before it goes into the dough: warm a small amount of milk, add the saffron strands, and leave for 10 to 15 minutes. The milk turns deep orange and the flavour becomes fuller. This step makes a real difference to the colour and fragrance of the finished buns. The dough is soft and enriched, similar to a brioche, and benefits from a stand mixer, though it can be made by hand with patient kneading.
Bloom the saffron and make the dough

Warm 200 ml milk until hand-hot. Add half a teaspoon of loosely packed saffron strands and leave to steep for 15 minutes; the milk will turn vivid orange. In a large bowl or the bowl of a stand mixer, combine 500 g plain flour, 7 g fast-action yeast, 75 g caster sugar, and half a teaspoon of salt. Add the saffron milk, 1 large egg, and 75 g softened diced butter. Mix to a rough dough, then knead with the dough hook for 8 minutes on medium speed, or by hand for 12 minutes, until smooth, soft, and slightly tacky but not sticky. Cover and leave to bulk ferment at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.

Line two baking trays with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces. Roll each piece into a rope about 30 cm long, no wider than 1 cm at the centre. Coil each end in opposite directions toward the centre to form an S shape. Place on the trays, spaced well apart. Press a raisin into the centre of each coil if using. Cover loosely and prove for 35 to 45 minutes until noticeably puffed.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Brush each bun gently with beaten egg. Bake for 9 to 12 minutes until pale gold. They should not darken: lussekatter are paler than most enriched buns. Cool for 5 minutes before serving. Best eaten the day they are made.

items.problem: The buns are pale and the saffron colour is faint
items.solution: The saffron was not steeped long enough, or the quantity was too small. Give it a full 15 minutes in warm (not boiling) milk. More saffron will intensify both colour and flavour.

items.problem: The S shape uncoiled during baking
items.solution: The ropes were not coiled tightly enough. Roll each rope thin and coil firmly, pressing the end slightly into the dough to anchor it. Resting on the tray before baking also helps the shape hold.
